edge: Large language models can solve tasks that were not present in the training set.
This capability is believed to be due to in-context learning and skill composition.
This capability is believed to be due to in-context learning and skill composition.
In this work, we study the emergence of in-context learning and skill composition.
we study the emergence of in-context learning and skill composition in a collection of modular arithmetic tasks.
We use some of these tasks for pre-training and the rest for out-of-distribution testing.
We empirically show that a GPT-style transformer exhibits a transition from in-distribution to out-of-distribution generalization as the number of pre-training tasks increases.

We find that the smallest model capable of out-of-distribution generalization requires two transformer blocks, while for deeper models, the out-of-distribution generalization phase is transient, necessitating early stopping.
Finally, we perform an interpretability study of the pre-trained models, revealing highly structured representations in both attention heads and MLPs; and discuss the learned algorithms.
Notably, we find an algorithmic shift in deeper models, as we go from few to many in-context examples.
LLMs can perform a variety of tasks upon appropriate prompting or fine-tuning, which is usually achieved via in-context learning.
LLMs learn simple tasks and compose those skills to form complex skills, demonstrating skill composition.
LLMs exhibit emergent capabilities, showing sudden emergence of new complex skills as a function of scale.
The sudden performance improvements of emergent capabilities are attributed to scale, including model size, compute, or data.
LLMs show grokking on algorithmic tasks, resulting from the model learning structured representations.
LLMs exhibit performance improvements attributed to mechanisms such as learning structured representations or other factors.
Investigation of emergence of in-context learning on linear regression tasks provides inspiration for examining skill composition.
Learning tasks related to linear function z = a x + b y mod p over Zp are introduced in the context of the autoregressive model.
The main objective of the algorithmic dataset is to probe how the autoregressive model utilizes the tasks it has learnt during training to solve the new tasks.
The algorithmic dataset is related to the autoregressive model (AM) through its main objective.
The autoregressive model (AM) learns tasks during training to solve new tasks.

The model performs on tasks and examples from the training set.
The model has four different phases.

The model generalizes on unseen examples (x, y) from the training set but does not generalize on unseen examples or tasks. This highlights the limitation of the model in directly generalizing to unseen tasks.
The model generalizes on unseen examples (x, y) but not on unseen tasks (a, b). This indicates the specific nature of generalization for in-distribution data.
The model generalizes on unseen tasks (a, b) but only for examples (x, y) it has seen during training. This highlights the memorization nature of the model for out-of-distribution tasks.
The model generalizes on unseen tasks (a, b) for seen as well as unseen examples (x, y). This showcases the broader generalization capability of the model for out-of-distribution tasks.

Representations developed by one of the attention heads in the first layer are projections of the embedding of a pair of numbers onto the two largest principal components (PCs) of the internal representation formed after passing through the attention layer and projection matrix. This highlights the role of attention heads in creating meaningful representations.

First 3 principal components (PCs) of embeddings can separate log27-annotated numbers into even/odd planes, with 0 sandwiched between them. This demonstrates the ability of embeddings to capture certain patterns in the data.

The solution found by the AM after optimization is qualitatively different from the linear regression cases studied before. AM develops an emergent first-order optimization method that minimizes an emergent quadratic loss function. This showcases the unique approach of AM in solving optimization tasks.
The quadratic loss function is mentioned as a way to solve the regression problem in the context.
A single linear attention layer is shown to solve the regression problem efficiently.
The addition of extra layers and non-linearities slightly modifies the gradient descent in the process.
AM learns how to solve pre-training tasks initially before moving on to develop generalizing solutions by combining the solved tasks.
The structure of the algorithmic dataset is illustrated along with the main findings.
A generalization transition is observed in the number of few-shot examples at inference time as the number of examples grows.

The presentation by Kirsch et al. showed how task diversity and model size affect the ICL performance for unseen tasks.
Raventos et al. investigated the relation between task diversity and out-of-distribution ICL ability on linear regression tasks.
Lin and Lee identified two operating modes in ICL using a mixture of linear regression tasks.
It was shown by Boix-Adserà et al. that with enough pre-training data, a transformer model can perform abstract reasoning that a MLP cannot do.
Guo et al. showed that transformers can use lower layers to memorize and upper layers to perform ICL in a feature regression setting.
It was found that ICL is a transient phase from the optimization point of view and goes away once the model is over-trained.
Hendel et al., Liu et al. showed that language models form in-context vectors, which can be used to steer model predictions.
Power et al. discovered Grokking, where models trained on modular.
arithmetic datasets have an abrupt change from random guessing to generalization on the test set way after the model memorized the training set.
Gromov showed that for modular addition tasks, models learned to map integers to Fourier features to solve modular arithmetic tasks.
Nanda et al. showed that for modular addition tasks, models learned to map integers to Fourier features to solve modular arithmetic tasks.
Gu et al. showed that for modular addition tasks, models learned to map integers to Fourier features to solve modular arithmetic tasks.
Liu et al. showed that grokking is related to learning highly structural features, and the grokking transition can be explained by a toy model.
Zhong et al. showed that there is more than one algorithm that a model can implement to solve modular addition.
Doshi et al. showed that corruption of the label does not prevent the models from finding a generalizing solution.
Doshi et al. showed that MLP and transformer models can solve a specific family of modular polynomial tasks by bijectively mapping them to modular addition tasks.
Elhage et al. showed that transformers can form induction heads that predict the next token in a sequence by identifying and copying patterns from earlier in the sequence.
Olsson et al. showed that transformers can form induction heads that predict the next token in a sequence by identifying and copying patterns from earlier in the sequence.
Nichani et al. showed theoretically and empirically how disentangled transformers learn causal structures from in-context Markov chains by forming induction heads.

node_2: figure 5: d = 4 and d = 2 models’ performance on k-shot inference
edge: Models' predictions on out-of-distribution data are compared with the performance of d = 4 and d = 2 models on k-shot inference tasks, highlighting the versatility of models in different scenarios.
The algorithms implemented by the models for out-of-distribution generalization include Ratio Matching, which involves a specific condition to generate predictions based on in-context examples.

The algorithm only works when y/x = yi/xi mod p holds for at least one of the in-context examples. Modular Regression involves finding {c1, c2, . . . , ck} such that the prediction is z =(cid:80)ki=1 cizi mod p.

The d = 4 model can combine the in-context examples using the Modular Regression algorithm, leading to near-perfect o.o.d. generalization. On the other hand, the d = 2 model still uses Ratio Matching and shows sub-optimal performance.
The d = 4 model exhibits an algorithmic shift from Ratio Matching to Modular Regression, whereas the d = 2 model shows sub-optimal performance due to limited capacity.
The d = 4 models exhibits an algorithmic shift from Ratio Matching to Modular Regression as the number of in-context examples increases, while the d = 2 models shows no such shift.
To implement these algorithms, the model needs to perform linear operations over GF(p).
In addition to copying information, which is readily implemented by attention heads, various skills are required for the model to operate effectively.
Modular Map is used to encode the tokens so that operations over GF(p) can be easily implemented.
Multiplication is a necessary skill to rescale in-context examples in both algorithms described.
Addition is a necessary skill for combining in-context examples in the Modular Regression algorithm.

The models transition to a qualitatively different algorithmic approach, where the task vector is determined at inference time.
We have examined the learnt representations and shown that qualitatively different circuits are formed in different phases.
In the o.o.d. generalization phase, we explain the learnt algorithms and highlight an in-context algorithmic shift in deeper models.
White-box interpretability analysis of deeper models has proved to be much more difficult than that of shallower models.
Consequently, we still do not understand the role of every individual component of the network in the deeper cases.
